title: Isolating Stem Cells from Mouse Hair Follicles: A Tool for Regenerative Research
description:
{"blocks":[{"key":"b4cru","text":"The hair follicle (HF) is a highly dynamic
organ that serves as an ideal model for studying adult stem cells (SCs). The
permanent portion of the HF, known as the bulge, houses multipotent stem cells
that play a crucial role in tissue homeostasis and regeneration. These
bulge-derived stem cells can differentiate into various cell types, including
interfollicular epidermis, sebaceous glands, and corneal epithelial cells,
under appropriate conditions. Here, we present a detailed protocol for the
isolation and enrichment of hair follicle stem cells (HFSCs) from mouse hair
follicles using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS). This method employs
specific markers such as CD34 and integrin alpha 6 (α6) to reliably identify
and isolate HFSC populations. The protocol begins with the dissection and
enzymatic digestion of mouse skin to release individual hair follicles,
followed by preparation of a single-cell suspension. FACS is then utilized to
separate HFSCs based on marker expression, yielding a highly enriched cell
population suitable for downstream applications. These cells can be used for in
vitro studies to investigate mechanisms of stem cell multipotency,
quiescence, and activation, as well as in vivo grafting models to study
their regenerative potential.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":1108,"length":8},{"style":"italic","offset":1217,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines:
{"blocks":[{"key":"cumrd","text":"To conduct this protocol, strict adherence
to the recommendations and standards outlined and approved by the Institutional
Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) guidelines is essential. These guidelines
are designed to ensure the humane treatment, welfare, and ethical use of
animals in research.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
